title: A Salty Surprise | JWST Detects Vaporized Salt Clouds on the Pink Planet GJ 504b
subtitle: Using the James Webb Space Telescope NIRSpec instrument, astronomers detected vaporized sodium chloride and potassium chloride clouds blanketing the magenta exoplanet GJ 504b, rewriting models of how massive alien worlds evolve.
thumbnail_alt: Artistic rendering of the magenta exoplanet GJ 504b with swirling salt clouds against deep space

metadata.title: James Webb Salt Clouds Pink Planet | GJ 504b Atmosphere Discovery
metadata.description: JWST detected vaporized salt clouds on exoplanet GJ 504b, the famous Pink Planet. Sodium chloride and potassium chloride found in its atmosphere by NIRSpec. New exoplanet chemistry data.

images.alt: Vaporized sodium chloride clouds on exoplanet GJ 504b the Pink Planet

content_html:
Ever since its initial discovery in 2013, the gas giant exoplanet GJ 504b has captivated the astronomical community. Affectionately dubbed the Pink Planet, this giant world glows with a striking dark magenta hue, a visual side-effect of heat trapped within its young, newly formed atmosphere. But while its color has always made it a fan favorite, its atmospheric chemistry has remained a profound mystery. That mystery just took a highly unexpected turn. In a series of breakthrough observations published this week, astronomers using the James Webb Space Telescope have officially detected the distinct chemical signature of vaporized salt clouds blanketing the magenta giant. The findings challenge everything planetary scientists thought they knew about how these massive, isolated alien worlds evolve.

Piercing the Magenta Haze | NIRSpec Observations

Located roughly 57 light-years from Earth in the constellation Virgo, GJ 504b is a massive planet clocking in at roughly four times the mass of Jupiter. Because it orbits its host star at an immense distance, nearly nine times farther than Neptune orbits our Sun, it has been incredibly difficult to study using traditional ground-based telescopes. To cut through the planet thick infrared signature, researchers turned to the Space Telescope Science Institute data pipeline and JWST Near-Infrared Spectrograph.

When the light from the planet atmosphere was broken down into its component wavelengths, scientists did not find the standard cocktail of methane and water vapor they expected. Instead, they spotted massive, unmistakable absorption lines belonging to sodium chloride (table salt) and potassium chloride. The presence of these vaporized salts at such high concentrations is unprecedented in exoplanet atmospheric chemistry and represents the first confirmed detection of mineral-vapor clouds on a directly imaged exoplanet.

How a Planet Rains Salt | The Vaporization Mechanism

On Earth, salt is a solid mineral safely contained within our oceans and crust. But GJ 504b is a world of extremes. Because the planet is relatively young at around 160 million years old, it is still radiating intense, scorching residual heat from its initial gravitational collapse. With atmospheric temperatures hovering at a blistering 460 degrees Celsius (860 degrees Fahrenheit), the physics of weather are completely warped. Deep within the crushing high-pressure layers of the planet lower atmosphere, solid salts are superheated until they vaporize into a gas. Strong convective atmospheric currents push this vaporized salt upward into the cooler upper layers of the stratosphere. As the gas cools, it condenses into thick, reflective clouds of crystalline salt particles, which then rain back down into the lower depths of the planet.

We are not looking at water clouds or even ammonia clouds, explained Dr. Mark Gorski, an atmospheric modeler involved in the data analysis. We are looking at an alien sky dominated by rock-forming minerals that have been completely vaporized. It is a beautifully hostile environment. This mechanism of vaporized mineral condensation has been theorized for ultra-hot exoplanets but never confirmed at the scale and composition observed on GJ 504b.

Rewriting the Exoplanet Rulebook | Implications for Planet Formation

The presence of such high concentrations of sodium chloride suggests that the Pink Planet has a much higher metallicity, an astronomical term for elements heavier than hydrogen and helium, than a planet of its size should have. According to current core-accretion models, gas giants forming so far away from their parent stars should be composed almost entirely of light, pristine gases. The high volume of heavy vaporized salts suggests that GJ 504b either formed much closer to its star and violently migrated outward, or it swallowed an incredibly high volume of rocky, mineral-rich asteroids during its cosmic infancy. Both scenarios challenge existing formation models and suggest that the range of possible exoplanet chemistries is far wider than previously assumed.

For related coverage of JWST discoveries and exoplanet science, see OzoneNews reporting on cutting-edge space science and NASA mission updates. The salty clouds of GJ 504b serve as a vivid reminder that the universe routinely creates environments far weirder than anything found in our own solar system.
